content.text: Governance in Cowork means having clear, enforced answers to three questions: who is permitted to do what, how are changes made safely, and how do we know what actually happened. Without governance, a growing library of shared goals becomes an opaque system where nobody can explain why a connector has broad access, who approved a goal that sends external email, or when an instruction set was last reviewed.
Cowork's governance model is a three-layer
policy stack

: organization-level controls set the floor, workspace-level controls add team-specific restrictions, and goal-level controls add the most granular scoping. Each layer can only restrict, never expand, the layer above it.

The pyramid above shows the three layers and the primary controls available at each level. Acceptable-use controls, change-management controls, and audit controls each span multiple layers — the diagram shows which layer is the primary enforcement point for each.
The Policy Stack

Understanding which layer governs which decision is the starting point for designing an effective governance structure. A common mistake is trying to govern everything at the goal level — writing long, detailed instruction sets in every goal to encode policy that belongs in the workspace or org layer.
Org-Level: The Compliance Floor

Org-level controls are set once and apply to all workspaces. They encode what the organization as a whole has decided is acceptable use of Cowork, regardless of which team is doing the work. Typical org-level controls:
Prohibited action types:

a list of action categories that no goal in the org may take without explicit org-admin approval (e.g., bulk external email to more than 50 recipients, write access to HR systems, access to financial records not owned by the goal creator's team)
Data classification enforcement:
a mapping of data categories (PII, health data, financial records, attorney-client privilege) to the connector types and approval levels required to touch them
Connector allow-list:
the set of external services that may be authorized as connectors at all — prevents rogue workspace admins from authorizing an unsanctioned service
Data residency:
which geographic region processes and logs Cowork data; required for EU (GDPR), APAC, or sectoral compliance frameworks
Workspace-Level: Team Defaults

Workspace-level controls set the default behaviour for all goals in the workspace. They are set by workspace admins and apply unless a goal's configuration is more restrictive. Typical workspace-level controls:
Control
Example value
Effect
default_mode
sandbox_first
All new goals run sandbox before live; goal authors can only restrict further (e.g., require approval on sandbox too), not relax
require_approval
["external_send", "file_delete"]
These action types always need human approval in this workspace; goal cannot remove this requirement
connector_scope_policy
explicit_only
Goals must name specific folders/channels; wildcard scope ("all Drive") is rejected
approval_timeout
If an approval prompt is not responded to within 24h, the goal pauses rather than auto-approving or rejecting
max_goal_schedule_frequency
daily
No goal in the workspace may be scheduled more frequently than once per day
Goal-Level: Specific Constraints
Goal-level controls are the most granular and only affect one goal. They cannot relax workspace policy but can add stricter requirements:

A goal can require approval for actions that the workspace auto-approves — e.g., the workspace allows auto-approved Drive writes, but this specific goal requires human approval because it writes to a folder used by external partners

A goal can restrict its connector scope below what the workspace connector grants — e.g., the workspace Drive connector covers /Projects/, but this goal only reads /Projects/Q1/ to prevent accidental access to later quarters
A goal can specify a tighter sandbox-review requirement — e.g., the workspace uses sandbox_first for all goals, but this goal additionally requires two independent approvers before live execution
Acceptable-Use Policy

An acceptable-use policy (AUP) for Cowork defines what goals employees may create, what data those goals may access, and what actions those goals may take. A well-written AUP removes ambiguity about edge cases before they become incidents.
Core AUP Sections
A minimal Cowork AUP for a mid-size organization should address:

Permitted goal types: what categories of work goals may automate (research, summarization, scheduling, internal notifications) versus what is off-limits without explicit approval (customer communication on behalf of the company, financial transactions, personnel-related actions)
Data access rules: which data categories each connector type may access; who may authorize a connector that touches regulated data; what data may not be processed by Cowork goals at all

External communication rules: when goals may send email, Slack messages, or calendar invites to external parties; whether external communication requires approval in all cases or only above certain thresholds
Personal vs. shared goal rules: whether personal goals may use workspace connectors; what organizational data may appear in personal goal instruction sets
Review and approval: who must review a new shared goal before it is activated in production; what the review process looks like for goals that request broader scope than the workspace default
Encoding the AUP in Workspace Policy
Once the AUP is written, encode its rules in the workspace policy configuration so they are machine-enforced rather than honor-system:

# Workspace policy encoding an AUP (simplified)

# AUP rule: "External email requires manager approval"
require_approval:
  - external_email_send  # enforces the rule at the connector layer

# AUP rule: "No bulk operations without explicit scope"
connector_scope_policy: explicit_only  # rejects wildcard scope

# AUP rule: "All new goals are sandbox-first for first month"
default_mode: sandbox_first

# AUP rule: "No goal may run more than once per hour"
max_goal_schedule_frequency: hourly

# AUP rule: "Goals touching PII require two approvers"
pii_connector_approval_requirement: dual_approver

The measure of a good AUP is not its length — it is whether the rules in the document match the controls configured in the workspace. An AUP that says 'external email requires approval' but where the workspace has auto_approve: ["external_send"] is not governance; it is theatre.
Change Management for Cowork Goals

In most organizations, production software changes go through a review process before deployment. Cowork shared goals — which run autonomously and may touch external systems — deserve the same discipline. A goal whose instruction set is changed ad-hoc without review is a change-management gap.
Goal Versioning

Cowork stores the full version history of every goal's instruction set. Every time the instruction set is saved, a new version is recorded with a timestamp and the identity of the person who saved it. This provides an audit trail for goal changes and makes rollback possible — any version from the past 30 days can be restored by the goal owner or a workspace admin.
Change Review Process for Shared Goals
Shared goals in production should follow a change process analogous to a pull request:
The goal owner creates a new version of the instruction set and marks it as a 'draft' — the current live version continues running

The draft is reviewed by a second workspace member (ideally the workspace admin or a nominated reviewer), who checks: is the scope still appropriate, is the new behaviour what the goal description says, has the sandbox been run with the new instruction set
The reviewer approves the draft; the goal owner promotes it to the live version

The first live run after a change is always reviewed — even if the goal normally runs auto-approved, the first post-change run produces a sandbox report sent to the reviewer for comparison with previous runs
Emergency Changes

Some changes need to go live quickly — a goal is producing wrong output, a connector's endpoint changed, a data source moved. Emergency changes follow an abbreviated process: the change is made immediately, but the change is logged with an explanation ("emergency: connector endpoint changed"), and a formal post-change review is scheduled within 24 hours. Emergency changes that skip the sandbox step must be flagged explicitly in the action log.
Data Handling and Privacy Controls

Cowork goals read organizational data through connectors. Depending on what those connectors are authorized to access, goals may process data that is subject to privacy regulations (GDPR, CCPA, HIPAA) or contractual confidentiality obligations.
Data Minimization
Privacy frameworks universally require processing the minimum data necessary for the stated purpose. In Cowork terms, data minimization means:
Scope to the minimum necessary resource:
a goal that summarizes a weekly sales report should have Drive access scoped to that specific report file, not the entire Sales folder
Read only what you process:
if a goal generates a meeting summary from calendar events, it should not have access to email — even if the user has both authorized
Do not log personal data unnecessarily:

goal instruction sets that instruct Cowork to 'summarize each person's contribution' will include personal data in the action log; if this is not needed for audit purposes, use aggregate rather than individual-level framing
Data Subject Rights

Under GDPR and CCPA, individuals have the right to know what data is held about them and to request deletion. If Cowork processes personal data (employee information, customer records, contact lists), the organization must be able to respond to data subject requests. The Cowork action log — which records what data was read — supports this: an org admin can search the log for a specific individual's identifier to identify which goals processed their data and when.
Connector Authorization Scope and Privacy

OAuth connectors authorized for Google Drive or Gmail are authorized against a specific Google account. If that account contains personal data — employee performance reviews, confidential personnel correspondence — the connector's scope must be explicitly restricted to exclude that data. A broad connector scope in an HR-adjacent workspace is a GDPR risk even if no goal currently reads the sensitive folders, because a future goal author could inadvertently include them.
The Governance Review Cycle

Governance is not a one-time configuration activity — it is an ongoing practice. A governance review cycle schedules periodic checks that keep the policy stack current with the organization's actual use of Cowork.
Quarterly Review Checklist
Review area
What to check
Responsible party
Goal inventory
Every shared goal has a current owner; dormant goals (no runs in 90 days) are archived or documented
Workspace admin
Connector scope audit
Each workspace connector's authorized scope is still the minimum needed for active goals
Workspace admin
Policy alignment
Workspace policy configuration matches the current AUP; any AUP updates since last review are reflected in policy
Org admin
Role review
All users with Admin role are still in that role for a documented reason; leavers have been offboarded
Org admin + workspace admin
Audit log review
Any scope_violation_attempt or injection_pattern_flagged events from the quarter have been investigated and resolved
Workspace admin
Approval effectiveness
Approval prompts are being genuinely reviewed (not rubber-stamped); rejection rate > 0% is a healthy signal
Workspace admin
Incident Register

Keep an incident register for Cowork — a simple shared document with one row per significant event: the date, the goal involved, what happened, what the root cause was, and what change was made in response. This register serves two purposes: it is evidence of a functioning governance process for compliance reviewers, and it is institutional memory that prevents the same class of incident from recurring.

A quarterly review that finds nothing to change is not evidence that governance is working — it may be evidence that the review is not thorough enough. Expect to find at least one dormant goal, one over-broad connector scope, or one policy gap in every quarterly cycle.
Audit Log Deep-Dive
The Cowork audit log is the source of truth for what happened in the system. Knowing how to read and query it is a core governance skill — both for routine monitoring and for incident investigation.
Log Entry Anatomy

{
  "timestamp": "2026-06-30T14:22:01Z",
  "org_id": "org_acme",
  "workspace": "engineering",
  "project": "incident-response",
  "goal_id": "goal_7k9x2p",
  "goal_name": "weekly-postmortem-drafter",
  "actor": "priya@acme.com",
  "actor_role": "member",
  "event_type": "goal_run_completed",
  "run_mode": "live",
  "actions_taken": [
    {
      "action": "drive_read",
      "resource": "/Engineering-Docs/Incidents/2026-06/",
      "item_count": 4
    },
    {
      "action": "drive_write",
      "resource": "/Engineering-Docs/Postmortems/2026-06-30.doc",
      "bytes_written": 14200
    },
    {
      "action": "slack_send",
      "channel": "#eng-weekly",
      "message_length": 480
    }
  ],
  "approval_events": [],
  "duration_seconds": 42,
  "session_id": "sess_m2kx9"
}
Key Fields for Governance Queries
Field
Governance use
event_type
Filter for scope_violation_attempt, injection_pattern_flagged, approval_rejected, connector_authorized to find high-priority events
actor
Track all actions taken by a specific user — useful for off-boarding reviews and data subject rights requests
goal_id
Get the full run history of one goal — useful for drift investigation and post-incident analysis
actions_taken[].resource
Find all accesses to a specific file or folder — useful for data breach scoping
run_mode
Filter for live vs sandbox to understand how often a goal actually took real-world actions
approval_events
Empty list = auto-run; populated = human reviewed; find rejection patterns to tune approval gates
Common Audit Queries
Three queries that should be part of every quarterly review:
All scope_violation_attempt events in the past 90 days: any result is a flag — investigate the goal and source data for prompt injection or goal drift
All connector_authorized events in the past 90 days: verify each authorization was intended and scoped correctly; remove any connector no longer needed by active goals
All goals run by users who have left the organization: goals should have been transferred or archived on departure; any live run by a deprovisioned account indicates an SCIM gap
Compliance Certifications and Shared Responsibility

Cowork operates on a shared-responsibility model for compliance: Anthropic is responsible for the platform's own security and compliance posture; the customer organization is responsible for how they configure and use the platform.
What Anthropic Provides
SOC 2 Type II:

Cowork's infrastructure and operational processes are audited annually against the SOC 2 trust service criteria (security, availability, confidentiality). The report is available to enterprise customers under NDA.
Data encryption:
data at rest and in transit is encrypted using AES-256 and TLS 1.3 respectively; connector OAuth tokens are encrypted at rest
Data residency options:
enterprise accounts can select EU or US data processing regions to support GDPR adequacy requirements
Penetration testing:
annual third-party penetration testing of the Cowork platform; results inform the security roadmap
What the Customer Configures
Platform-level certifications do not automatically make your use of Cowork compliant. Customer responsibilities include:
Data classification and connector scope:
ensuring that connectors are not authorized to access regulated data categories unless the goal has a documented legal basis to process that data
Access control:
configuring SSO, maintaining accurate role assignments, and off-boarding users promptly so that deprovisioned accounts cannot access Cowork goals or action logs
Retention configuration:
setting audit log retention to match the organization's record-keeping obligations; the default 12-month retention may not satisfy certain industry-specific requirements
AUP and governance document:
GDPR, HIPAA, and similar frameworks require documented policies for AI-assisted data processing; Anthropic's compliance certification covers the platform, not the customer's policies
Data subject rights procedures:
the customer must define and operate the process for responding to access, correction, and deletion requests for personal data that Cowork goals have processed

A SOC 2 report from Anthropic shows that the platform is run securely. It does not show that your goals are scoped correctly, that your AUP is enforced, or that your users are processing data in a privacy-compliant manner. Those are your configurations to make and maintain.
Common Governance Anti-Patterns
Most governance failures in Cowork deployments follow a small set of recurring patterns. Recognizing them early is cheaper than investigating an incident caused by them.
Anti-pattern
What it looks like
Why it fails
Fix
The orphan goal
A shared goal that runs reliably but whose original creator has left; nobody owns it
No one reviews sandbox reports, responds to approval prompts, or updates the instruction set when the source data changes
Assign ownership in the governance doc; transfer ownership on off-boarding; archive dormant goals
The master connector
A workspace connector authorized for 'all Drive' or 'all Slack' because it was 'easier to set up'
Any goal in the workspace — including future goals not yet written — can access all data the connector can reach
Scope each connector to the minimum needed for active goals; review quarterly
Policy theatre
The AUP says external email requires approval, but the workspace has auto_approve: [external_send]
The policy is unenforceable; any member can create a goal that sends external email without review
Encode every AUP rule in workspace policy configuration; verify alignment quarterly
The rubber-stamp approver
An approval gate exists, but the approver clicks approve immediately without reading the sandbox report
The approval gate provides no safety value; a prompt injection or drift event will not be caught
Monitor approval-to-response time; train approvers; consider removing gates that are never genuinely reviewed and replacing with sandbox-only
The stale governance doc
The governance document describes connectors and policies that were changed six months ago; the document hasn't been updated
Auditors and new team members work from incorrect information; actual policy diverges from documented policy
Treat the governance doc like code: update it when policy changes, review it quarterly, put it in version control
Writing the Cowork Governance Document

A governance document brings together policy, process, and accountability in a single reference. It does not need to be long — three to five pages is typically sufficient for a 20–50 person team. The audience is the workspace admin, auditors, and new team members setting up their first shared goal.
Recommended Sections
Scope and purpose:
what Cowork is used for in this organization, what workspaces exist, who is accountable for each
Acceptable use:
what goals are permitted, what data may be accessed, what actions require pre-approval — should match the workspace policy configuration exactly
Connector registry:
a table of all authorized connectors, the service account used to authorize each one, the scope granted, the date authorized, and the goals that use it
Change process:
how a new shared goal is reviewed before activation; how changes to live goals are reviewed; the emergency change procedure
Review schedule:
when quarterly reviews happen, who conducts them, and where the review output is recorded
Incident response:
the containment and investigation playbook; the incident register location; who to notify when an incident occurs
Summary
Governance in Cowork is the practice of maintaining clear, enforced, and audited answers to who can do what, how changes are made safely, and what actually happened.
Policy stack:
three layers (org, workspace, goal) where each layer can only restrict the one above; controls belong at the highest layer where they apply universally
Acceptable-use policy:
encodes the organization's decisions about permitted goal types, data access, and external actions; must be reflected in workspace policy configuration to be enforceable
Change management:
shared goals in production deserve a review process analogous to code review; every change is versioned and the first post-change run is reviewed
Data minimization:
connector scope should reflect the minimum data necessary; over-broad scope is a privacy risk even if no current goal reads the excess data
Quarterly review cycle:
goal inventory, connector scope audit, policy alignment check, role review, and audit log review — each quarter, with findings documented
Governance document:
the single reference that records what is authorized, who is accountable, how changes are made, and where incidents are tracked

Governance that exists only as a document is compliance-theatre. Governance that is encoded in workspace policy, enforced by the platform, reviewed quarterly, and updated in response to incidents is a genuine operational discipline — one that makes agentic AI safe to run at scale.
